title: Chapter 38: The Gala
content: The Annual Fashion Gala looks exactly the same as it did two months ago.

Same ice sculpture in the shape of the Mephisto MCN logo — that sleek M with the curling tale, get it, *tale*. Same champagne tower catching the light like a waterfall made of money. Same cluster of influencers near the entrance, performing their greetings for phones they're pretending not to check.
Same room.
Different me.

I'm wearing black. Not the algorithm's black — the silk cocktail dress that trended on #CreatorGala and made everyone look like they were cosplaying the same vampire. My black is different. A gown I picked myself. Simple. Severe. High collar, long sleeves, open back — because I wanted to feel the air on my spine, wanted to feel *something* instead of the numb hollow that used to live there. The fabric is matte, almost monastic, like something you'd wear to a funeral or a coronation.
Tonight is both.

I walk through the entrance and the room does the thing. You know the thing. Two hundred heads turning in sequence, like a wave at a stadium, except the sport is *me*. Half of them know the story by now — the contract, the soul theft, the court case that made the front page of every outlet from TechCrunch to the New York Times. The other half are about to find out.

I can smell the room as I step inside. Perfume — twelve different designer fragrances competing for dominance, none of them winning. And underneath that, the electricity. Not metaphorical. The venue's running enough LED rigs to power a small country, and the ozone from all that light mingles with the floral notes and the champagne and something else, something I can only describe as *beginning*.

I pick up a glass from a passing tray. The champagne is cold and sharp and tastes like an actual drink instead of a prop. I take a real sip. I can taste it. God, I can *taste* it. Two months of flavorless food and water that felt like sand, and now the bubbles are dancing across my tongue like they've got somewhere to be. I take another sip just to make sure.
Still real. Still mine.
Then I see him.

Mephisto is across the room, holding court near the ice sculpture like he always does. His human skin is flawless tonight — the kind of flawless that makes you realize every other face in the room is just *trying*. Dark hair swept back, jawline sharp enough to cut glass, and that suit. Italian. Probably hand-stitched by whichever tailor has been dressing demons since the Renaissance. He's smiling at something someone said, that easy charm radiating off him like heat off pavement in August.
He's beautiful.

I can see it now — really see it — the way the light catches the planes of his face, the way his presence bends the room around him like gravity. Two months ago, that beauty would have done its job. Would have made me lean in, made me want to be closer, made me sign things I shouldn't sign. That was the point. That was always the point.
But it doesn't work anymore.
He's beautiful the way a weapon is beautiful. The way a venomous snake has gorgeous scales. You can see it and not pick it up. You can admire the design and still walk the other way.

I own this room. Not metaphorically — I own the company that rented it, the company that hired the caterers, the company that carved its own logo into a block of ice. Fifty-one percent. A federal judge signed the paper yesterday, and 51% of everything Mephisto built belongs to me.
He's playing host at his own party, except it's not his party anymore.
The irony tastes better than the champagne.
I'm halfway through my second sip when he sees me. Our eyes lock across the room and something passes between us — not magic, not contract-bond, just recognition. Predator acknowledging predator.

He's walking toward me. The crowd parts for him without realizing they're doing it. That's his gift — making people get out of his way while thinking it was their idea. I don't move. I stand exactly where I am and I wait.

He stops three feet away. Close enough to be intimate. Far enough to be safe. He's smiling like nothing in the universe is wrong, like he didn't lose 51% of his empire yesterday, like he's not standing in a room full of people who are about to learn what he really is.
"Ms. Hart." His voice is warm and rich. Predictable. "You look... human tonight."

It's not a compliment. It's an assessment. He's cataloguing me — the black gown, the actual expression on my face, the fact that I'm standing like someone who belongs here instead of someone who's grateful to be invited. He's trying to figure out what I am now that the contract's been broken.
I smile back. Not the curated smile I used for content. Not the grateful smile I gave him at the 10M party when I was too hollowed out to know better. A real one. The kind that shows teeth.
"Mr. Mephisto." I let the name sit there, all five syllables of it. "You look... temporary."
His smile flickers.

Just for a second. Just a micro-expression, a twitch at the corner of his perfect mouth. But I catch it, and it's the most satisfying thing I've seen since the judge's gavel came down. He hasn't heard that word applied to him in millennia. He's eternal. He's absolute. He's been collecting souls since before the Sumerians learned to write, and now a twenty-three-year-old in a black dress just called him *temporary*.
I let that sit too.

He recovers fast. Credit where it's due — the demon's got composure. But he shifts closer, and I recognize the move. Same lean-in he used at the 10M party when he announced the grief deduction, when he took my ability to mourn my own mother and made it sound like a feature update. That proprietary closeness. That *I own the space you're breathing* energy.
"You think a court order changes anything?" His voice drops, just for me. Intimate. Dangerous. "I've been making deals since before your species invented writing. This is a setback. Nothing more."

My heart is hammering. I can feel it — the fear, real and physical, pressing against my sternum like something trying to get out. Two months ago, that fear would have made me smaller. Would have made me nod and laugh nervously and look for the exit.
But I can feel it now, and I don't flinch anyway.

"Here's what the court order does," I say, and my voice is steady. Steadier than I thought it would be. "It gives me fifty-one percent of your company. It gives me access to every file, every directory, every archived soul. It makes me your boss."
I pause. Let that land. Watch his eyes — black, ancient, bottomless as an oil spill — and I don't look away.
"And here's what I'm going to do with it."
Another pause. The room around us is still buzzing, still champagne and laughter and performative networking, but the space between us has gone quiet. Sacred. Like a church or a surrender.

"I'm going to release every archived emotion. Every stolen feeling. All 847,000 of them." The number is precise because I made Leo count them three times. "And then I'm going to shut down the extraction mechanism permanently."
His face doesn't change. But something behind his eyes does.
"Your harvest is over."

Something shifts in Mephisto's expression. Not anger — I was ready for anger. This is more interesting. He looks at me the way you'd look at a painting you can't decode, a sculpture that shouldn't be possible given the materials. Like I'm a problem he actually has to think about.
"You're magnificent," he says.
Not as flattery. Not as a line. As observation. The way you'd say *the sun is hot* or *the ocean is deep*. Simple. Factual. Almost clinical.
"The contract was supposed to hollow you." He's studying me now, genuinely studying, the way a scientist studies an anomaly. "Instead, it sharpened you. I created my own worst enemy."
There it is — the admission. Not remorse. Not regret. Just... appreciation. The way you'd appreciate an opponent who finally made the game interesting.
"You didn't create me," I say. "You harvested me. There's a difference."

He regards me for a long moment. The noise of the gala fills the silence between us — clinking glasses, practiced laughter, the bass thrum of a DJ who thinks they're more important than they are. And in the middle of all that noise, we're standing in a bubble of absolute stillness.
"You could have been my greatest acquisition," he says finally. Soft. Almost tender. Like he's mourning an investment.

I straighten my spine. I can feel myself standing at my full height for the first time in this entire story — not the slight hunch of someone trying to take up less space, not the practiced posture of a content creator angles-maximizing. Just me. Five foot six and every inch claimed.
"I was never yours to acquire."
I turn my back on him.

That's it. That's the move. Not a counter-argument, not a threat, not a dramatic exit line. Just — walking away. From a demon who's been inside my head, inside my soul, inside the architecture of my emotions for two straight months. From the most powerful being I've ever encountered. From the creature who turned me inside out and wore me like a coat.
I walk away.
And he lets me.

I can feel him standing there. I don't turn to check — I don't need to. His eyes on my back, that black gaze tracking me across the room, recalculating, re-strategizing. A demon who respects you is a demon who's planning his next move. I know that. I'll deal with it later.
Right now, I have a room to address.

The stage is at the far end. Small, elegant, draped in black velvet — his aesthetic, always his aesthetic, dark and seductive. But I'm about to use his own stage against him, and the poetry of that is not lost on me.
I step up. The microphone is there, waiting, expectant. I tap it once. Feedback squeals through the room, and two hundred heads turn toward me.
The silence hits like a wave.

Two hundred influencers, two hundred content creators, two hundred people who've built their lives on never being caught off-guard — and every single one of them goes quiet. The DJ cuts the music. The ice sculpture melts, indifferent. And I can feel the warmth of the spotlight on my skin, golden and heavy, pressing into me like a hand on my shoulder.
"My name is Chloe Hart."
My voice carries. Clear. No trembling. No faltering. Just words, and they're mine.
"Two months ago, I signed a contract with this company that cost me my soul."

A murmur ripples through the crowd. Some of them knew. Some of them suspected. Some of them are hearing it for the first time, and I can see their faces cycling through confusion, recognition, horror.
"Yesterday, a federal court ruled that contract illegal."

The murmur becomes a rumble. Phones are coming out — recording, always recording. Good. I want this documented. I want this on every platform, every feed, every algorithm. I want the whole world to see what he built and what I'm about to tear down.
"Today, I own this company."

The rumble becomes a roar, and I let it. Let them process. Let them understand. Some of them are clutching their chests — Creators who felt the extraction, who knew something was wrong but couldn't name it, who chalked it up to burnout or depression or the general numbness of living online. They know now. They *know*.
"And I have one announcement."
I look down at my phone. Leo's text is sitting there, three words that took him six weeks to make reality:
**unDRM permanent. Release ready.**

My thumb hovers over the reply field. 847,000 emotions. 847,000 stolen pieces of people's interior lives, locked in Mephisto's servers, waiting. Grief and joy and fury and tenderness and every shade of feeling that makes a person *a person*. All of them about to go home.

"Every archived emotion that was stolen from every creator will be returned. Tonight." I look up, scan the crowd. Find the faces that are scared and the ones that are hopeful and the ones that are both. "Right now."
I type one word.
**RELEASE**
The phone buzzes. Leo's reply is instant: **Done.**
And across the city — across the country, across the server farms and the cloud banks and the digital architecture that Mephisto spent millennia building — 847,000 people wake up.

I can't see them. I can't hear them. But I can *feel* it, somehow, a shift in the atmosphere, a pressure change, like the moment before a storm breaks. Somewhere, a mother feels the full weight of her grief for the first time in years and finally, *finally* gets to cry. Somewhere, a comedian laughs at his own joke and means it. Somewhere, a man stands in his kitchen and feels the warmth of the morning sun and doesn't know why he's crying but he's crying and it's *real*.
847,000 people, feeling again. Tonight. Now.

The room is chaos. Crying and shouting and people holding each other and people staring at their phones in disbelief. Security is somewhere, probably, and there are definitely going to be lawsuits — Mephisto has 90 days to appeal, and a demon with 90 days is a demon with a plan.
But right now, in this moment, with the spotlight warm on my face and the sound of 200 people cracking open like eggs ringing in my ears — right now, I can feel the full weight of what I've done.
Not the victory. Not the satisfaction.
The weight.
847,000 souls, returned. A demon, humbled but not destroyed. A company, mine but broken. A future, uncertain but *mine*.
I set the microphone back in its stand. Step down from the stage. Walk through the crowd — they're too stunned to stop me, too overwhelmed to do anything but feel.
And for the first time in two months, I walk out of a Mephisto event through the front door.
Head up. Heart full. Soul — my own.
